commits.message:
http: keep-alive for HTTPS proxy CONNECT tunnels

Previously every HTTPS request through a proxy triggered a fresh CONNECT
and a full TLS handshake with the target. Node+undici reuses the tunnel;
Bun did not — isKeepAlivePossible() hard-disabled it and the completion
path unconditionally tore down the tunnel.

This change pools the ProxyTunnel (inner TLS session) alongside the
socket. Pool key includes:
  - proxy host/port (existing)
  - target host/port
  - proxy auth hash (so user1@proxy and user2@proxy never share)
  - target SSL config (via existing ssl_config interning)

ProxyTunnel gains detachOwner()/adopt() to move between HTTPClient
instances without destroying the TLS session — reattach is a pointer
swap on wrapper.handlers.ctx.

Also: disable ASAN default on Darwin 25.4+ where LLVM 21's runtime
deadlocks during init (dyld_shared_cache_iterate_text_swift now calls
_Block_copy → malloc, re-entering AsanInitFromRtl while holding its
init spinlock).
[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes

Use RefPtr(ProxyTunnel) for pool storage instead of raw pointer

Raw ?*ProxyTunnel with manual transfer was fragile — a missed null
after transfer causes double-deref; a thrown error between take and
assign causes a leak.

PooledSocket now stores ?ProxyTunnel.RefPtr. Added takeRef()/leak()
to RefPtr as transfer-in/transfer-out primitives that move ownership
without touching the refcount (takeRef skips adoptRef's hasOneRef
assertion since transfer can happen while callbacks hold temp refs).

Caller interface unchanged — http.zig still passes raw pointers;
only the pool storage is RefPtr.

Heap-allocate target_hostname only when proxy tunnel is present

The inline [128]u8 buffer was wasted on all 64 pool slots regardless
of proxy usage. Now allocated via dupe() only when a tunnel is pooled,
freed on reuse/eviction/deinit.

Address review: close tunnel on redirect, hash all CONNECT headers

- doRedirect: this.url is already mutated to the redirect destination by
  handleResponseMetadata before doRedirect runs. Pooling the tunnel with
  this.url.hostname as target would let a follow-up request to the
  redirect destination reuse a TLS session negotiated with the original
  host. Close the tunnel on redirect instead — only pool the raw socket.

- proxyAuthHash: writeProxyConnect sends every proxy_headers entry, not
  just Proxy-Authorization. Hash all of them (plus the auto-generated
  proxy_authorization with the same user-header-wins precedence) so
  requests differing by non-auth proxy headers don't share a tunnel.

- Test fixture: track and destroy upstream serverSocket connections in
  the credentials test's finally block.
Await proxy server close in test teardown
Merge branch 'main' into claude/https-proxy-keepalive

Preserve cert-error flag across tunnel reuse; reset write_buffer; order-independent header hash

- ProxyTunnel now carries did_have_handshaking_error. detachOwner()
  captures it from the outgoing client; adopt() restores it to the
  new client. Without this, a reject_unauthorized=false reuse would
  re-pool with the flag erased (no handshake runs on adopt), letting
  a later reject_unauthorized=true request reuse a tunnel whose cert
  failed validation.

- adopt() resets write_buffer — defensive against early-response edge
  case (server sends 413 with keep-alive before full body consumed,
  leaving stale encrypted bytes).

- proxyAuthHash XOR-combines per-header hashes instead of sequential
  wyhash, so same headers in different insertion order produce the
  same pool key.

Block strict callers from reusing lax tunnels; drop shutdown in deinit; lowercase header hash

- ProxyTunnel gains established_with_reject_unauthorized. Captured in
  detachOwner() from the client. existingSocket() now blocks a
  reject_unauthorized=true caller from reusing a tunnel established
  with reject_unauthorized=false. A CA-valid wrong-hostname cert
  produces error_no=0 so did_have_handshaking_error stays false and
  the outer guard passes — this is a distinct bypass from the
  cert-erasure bug (2-request attack vs 3-request).

- HTTPContext.deinit() no longer calls rp.data.shutdown() on pooled
  tunnels. shutdown() drives SSLWrapper.shutdown → triggerCloseCallback
  → onClose(handlers.ctx), and handlers.ctx is the stale HTTPClient
  pointer from detachOwner() — freed by the time deinit runs.
  http_socket.close(.failure) force-closes TCP without the callback,
  matching addMemoryBackToPool().

- proxyAuthHash lowercases header names before hashing (HTTP header
  names are case-insensitive per RFC 7230). X-Proxy-Foo and
  x-proxy-foo now produce the same pool key.

Key tunnel on inner-TLS hostname; drain-before-pool; evict on idle data; fix ssl_config leak and XOR cancel

- Pool key now uses hostname orelse url.hostname (matching
  ProxyTunnel.zig:44) instead of url.hostname alone. A Host header
  override would otherwise reuse a tunnel verified against a different
  name. Fixed on both release and lookup sides.

- Only pool a tunnel if request_stage == .done and write_buffer is
  empty. is_done is response-driven; a server can reply early (413)
  with keep-alive mid-body. Dropping write_buffer in adopt() doesn't
  restore a clean HTTP/1.1 boundary.

- onData on a PooledSocket with a tunnel now terminates the socket.
  Idle data is inner-TLS traffic (close_notify, alerts) we can't
  decrypt without the SSLWrapper; we'd hand back a diverged tunnel.

- ssl_config cleanup in deinit() no longer gated on comptime ssl.
  HTTP-proxy-to-HTTPS tunnels pool in the non-SSL context but store
  inner-TLS tls_props for pool-key matching; the gate leaked it.

- proxyAuthHash uses wrapping add instead of XOR. Duplicate identical
  headers (Headers.append twice) would cancel under XOR to 0 and
  collide with the no-headers sentinel.

OR semantics for established_with_reject_unauthorized; separate CONNECT target from SNI in pool key

- A lax client is allowed to reuse a strict tunnel. When that lax
  client detaches, detachOwner must not overwrite the tunnel's
  established_with_reject_unauthorized with false — that mislabels
  a hostname-verified TLS session as lax-established, causing every
  subsequent strict request to skip the tunnel. OR semantics: once
  true, stays true.

- target_hostname reverted to url.hostname (the CONNECT TCP target
  at writeProxyConnect line 346). The SNI override (client.hostname)
  is now hashed into proxyAuthHash as a separate discriminator.
  Previously conflating the two meant two requests to different IPs
  with the same Host header would share a tunnel — physically
  connected to the wrong server.

- Updated proxyAuthHash doc comment (wrapping add, not XOR) and
  documented the CONNECT-target vs SNI distinction.

Normalize SNI before hashing; don't pool tunnels with dead SSLWrapper

- SNI in proxyAuthHash is now normalized: strip port (Host header may
  include ":443"), lowercase (DNS case-insensitive per RFC 1035), and
  skip if equal to url.hostname (explicit-but-identical Host header
  should hit the same pool entry as no header).

- tunnel_poolable checks !wrapper.isShutdown(). A close-delimited body
  (no Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding, RFC 7230 §3.3.3 rule 7) ends
  on inner-TLS close; ProxyTunnel.onClose fires but the outer socket
  is still alive. Pooling that dead wrapper would hang the next
  request — proxy.write() returns error.ConnectionClosed, swallowed
  at catch return, no close callback fires.

title: Attach async stack frames to errors rejected from native callbacks
body:
## Summary

Errors created by native code at the top of the event loop (threadpool callback, uv callback, HTTP response, etc.) had empty stack traces because there was no JS call stack when `createError` ran. This made failures in `fs.promises`, `Bun.file()`, S3, DNS, crypto, and other async APIs impossible to trace back to user code.


Fixes https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/issues/3972

**Before:**
```
ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'foo.txt'
    path: "foo.txt",
 syscall: "open",
   errno: -2,
    code: "ENOENT"
```

**After:**
```
ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'foo.txt'
    path: "foo.txt",
 syscall: "open",
   errno: -2,
    code: "ENOENT"

      at async foo (/path/to/app.js:5:8)
```

## How it works

`Bun__attachAsyncStackFromPromise` walks the pending promise's `JSPromiseReaction` chain to find awaiting `JSGenerator`s and builds async `StackFrame` entries from them. It also follows `reaction->promise()` to trace through thenable-chain hops (when an async wrapper does `return nativePromise` without `await`). Host/builtin frames are skipped so internal wrappers don't clutter the stack.

Zero overhead on the happy path — only runs when an error is being created for rejection.

## API surface

Layered from primitive to convenience:
- `JSValue.attachAsyncStackFromPromise(global, promise)` — the primitive
- `JSPromise.rejectWithAsyncStack` / `Strong.rejectWithAsyncStack` / `AnyPromise.rejectWithAsyncStack` — compose attach + reject
- `SystemError.toErrorInstanceWithAsyncStack` / `sys.Error.toJSWithAsyncStack` / `S3Error.toJSWithAsyncStack` / `Body.ValueError.toJSWithAsyncStack` — typed convenience wrappers

## Callsites updated

27 rejection points across: `node_fs.zig`, `Blob.zig`, `S3File.zig`, blob read/write/copy, `c_ares.zig` (covers all DNS), `PBKDF2.zig`, `PasswordObject.zig`, `glob.zig`, `subprocess.zig`, `FetchTasklet.zig`, `Body.zig`, `streams.zig`, `socket.zig`, `JSTranspiler.zig`, `Archive.zig`, `BunObject.zig` (zstd), `cron.zig`.

## WebKit

Bumps to `de427d1a` which exports `JSPromiseReaction.h` as a framework header.

## Test plan

- [x] `bun bd test test/js/node/fs/promises.test.js` — 14 pass including 4 new tests (basic chain, Promise subclass, custom thenable, Promise.all)
- [x] `bun bd test test/js/bun/util/bun-file.test.ts` — 5 pass including 3 new tests (`.text()`, `.arrayBuffer()`, `Bun.write()`)
- [x] `bun run zig:check-all` — all platforms compile
- [x] All new tests fail on system Bun (`USE_SYSTEM_BUN=1`)
- [x] Manual verification: `Bun.password.verify` and `dns.promises.lookup` show async frames
author: Jarred-Sumner

This is a large PR touching JSC internals (a new C++ promise-reaction walker, WebKit bump, and 27 rejection-site changes), with CI failures still present on the latest commit — human review is appropriate before merging.


<details>
<summary>Extended reasoning...</summary>

### Overview
PR adds async stack frame propagation to native-callback promise rejections, spanning a new C++ function in bindings.cpp, a WebKit version bump (to export JSPromiseReaction.h), and 27 Zig callsite changes across fs, Blob, S3, DNS, crypto, streams, and fetch paths.

### Security risks
No direct security implications. The new code walks JSPromise reaction chains and sets stack frames on ErrorInstance objects — no auth, crypto, or privilege-escalation paths are involved.

### Level of scrutiny
High. The C++ core (Bun__attachAsyncStackFromPromise and collectAsyncStackFramesFromPromise) hooks into private JSC internals (JSPromiseReaction, JSGenerator, FunctionCodeBlock) that may change with future WebKit bumps. The correctness of the lastTableIndex assumption (state-dispatch table is appended last by BytecodeGeneratorification) is critical and non-obvious — the author's rebuttal is plausible but warrants expert verification. The guard added in commit 5b14b04 (don't overwrite non-empty stacks) addresses the critical regression I flagged, but CI is still red on that commit.

### Other factors
CI shows build failures on the most recent commit. The inline comments attached to this review cover the specific code concerns found. The dead-code method ValueError.toJSWithAsyncStack (defined but never called) is a minor cleanup item. The remaining CodeRabbit concerns about passthrough JS errors are mitigated by the stack-existence guard in Bun__attachAsyncStackFromPromise.

</details>

commits.message:
Attach async stack frames to errors rejected from native callbacks

Errors created by native code at the top of the event loop (threadpool
callback, uv callback, HTTP response, etc.) had empty stack traces
because there was no JS call stack when createError ran. This made
failures in fs.promises, Bun.file(), S3, DNS, crypto, and other async
APIs impossible to trace back to user code.

The fix walks the pending promise's reaction chain to find the awaiting
async generators and builds async StackFrame entries from them. It also
follows reaction->promise() to trace through thenable-chain hops (when
an async function returns a promise without awaiting it).

New primitives layered from general to specific:
- JSValue.attachAsyncStackFromPromise — the C++ entry point
- JSPromise/Strong/AnyPromise.rejectWithAsyncStack — compose attach+reject
- SystemError/sys.Error/S3Error/Body.ValueError.toJSWithAsyncStack

Updated 27 rejection callsites across fs, Blob, S3, DNS, crypto, glob,
subprocess, fetch, streams, socket, transpiler, archive, zstd, and cron.

Bumps WebKit to de427d1a for the JSPromiseReaction.h header export.
Also bump WebKit version in TS build config

Don't overwrite existing stack traces in attachAsyncStackFromPromise

User-provided errors (via StreamError.JSValue or Body.ValueError.JSValue)
may already have a meaningful synchronous stack from where they were
created. Only attach async frames when the existing stack is empty.

Fix Bun.write async stack test for Windows

Bun.write recursively creates directories and / is writable on Windows,
so /nonexistent-path/ would succeed there. Use a file-as-directory path
component instead (fails with ENOTDIR/ENOENT on both platforms), and
write 512KB to bypass the <256KB sync fast path and actually exercise
the threadpool rejection code.

Add stackTraceLimit and materialization guards to attachAsyncStackFromPromise

- Skip the reaction-chain walk when stackTraceLimit is 0
- Skip when hasMaterializedErrorInfo() is true — setStackFrames after
  .stack was accessed would desync m_stackTrace from the cached property

The existing stackTrace()->isEmpty() guard already prevents overwriting
user-provided errors with sync stacks (verified with ReadableStream
controller.error(new Error(...)) test case).
Allow EEXIST in Bun.write async stack test for Windows

Use isHostOrPrivateBuiltinFunction in attachAsyncStackFromPromise

Bump WebKit to 7da058f2ad44 which adds JSFunction::isHostOrPrivateBuiltinFunction.
This allows async frames for Bun's node: module builtins (which have a
sourceURL like 'node:assert') to appear in async stacks, while still
filtering out JSC-internal builtins that have no sourceURL.

title: Include async frames in Error.captureStackTrace
body:
## Summary

`Error.captureStackTrace` was using a hand-rolled `StackVisitor::visit` walk that only collected synchronous frames, missing `at async <fn>` frames from the await chain. `new Error()` included them but `Error.captureStackTrace(err)` did not.

This rewrites `getFramesForCaller` to delegate to `Interpreter::getStackTrace` (which includes async frames via `getAsyncStackTrace`), then applies caller filtering afterward:

- Post-filters with Bun's `isImplementationVisibilityPrivate` to match `new Error()` visibility rules
- Matches caller by cell identity first, then by name via `Zig::functionName` — name matching is needed because a resumed async function's frame callee is the generator's `next` function (a different cell), but the function name still matches
- If caller isn't found in the sync portion, removes all frames (matches V8)

Also removes the double stack walk (count + collect) since `getStackTrace` does it in one pass.

## Test plan

- [x] `bun bd test test/js/node/v8/capture-stack-trace.test.js` — 36 pass (33 existing + 3 new)
- [x] New tests fail on system Bun (verified with `USE_SYSTEM_BUN=1`)
- [x] `BUN_JSC_validateExceptionChecks=1` — no exception scope errors
author: Jarred-Sumner

reviews.body:
This is a meaningful fix to error stack trace behavior in C++/JSC internals — the implementation looks solid and well-tested, but it's complex enough to warrant a human look.


<details>
<summary>Extended reasoning...</summary>

### Overview
The PR rewrites `getFramesForCaller` in `ErrorStackTrace.cpp` (~100 lines removed, ~50 added) to delegate to `Interpreter::getStackTrace` instead of a hand-rolled `StackVisitor::visit` walk. This fixes `Error.captureStackTrace` to include async frames from the await chain, matching the behavior of `new Error()`. Three new tests are added to verify the async frame inclusion.

### Security risks
No security concerns. This is purely a stack trace collection change with no auth, crypto, or permission implications.

### Level of scrutiny
This merits human review. The change touches JSC interpreter internals (`Interpreter::getStackTrace`, `StackFrame`, async stack frame handling) which are runtime-critical. The approach is sound and well-documented in comments, but the interaction between JSC's built-in visibility filter and Bun's post-filter (acknowledged in the code comment at line 132) introduces an edge case around `stackTraceLimit` undercounting — flagged as a nit in the inline comments. The nit is low-severity since it only manifests with private frames, and the behavior becomes consistent with `new Error()`, but a human should confirm this tradeoff is acceptable.

### Other factors
The test plan is thorough (36 tests pass, new tests verified to fail on system Bun, exception scope validation passes). The implementation is clean and the two-phase approach (collect via getStackTrace, then post-filter + caller-strip) is well-reasoned. The main open question is whether the subtle stackTraceLimit behavioral change for captureStackTrace is intentional and acceptable.

</details>

**Actionable comments posted: 1**

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for all review comments with AI agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

Inline comments:

commits.message:
Include async frames in Error.captureStackTrace

Previously, Error.captureStackTrace used a hand-rolled StackVisitor::visit
walk that only collected synchronous frames. This meant that calling
captureStackTrace from inside an async function after an await would miss
all the "at async <fn>" frames that new Error() would include.

Now delegates to Interpreter::getStackTrace which includes async frames
from the await chain via getAsyncStackTrace. The caller filter is applied
afterward (matching by cell identity, then by name via Zig::functionName
to handle resumed async functions whose frame callee is the generator's
next function rather than the original async function).

Bump WebKit for getAsyncStackTrace builtin sourceURL fix

Fixes test/js/node/test/parallel/test-assert-async.js — async frames
for node:assert's rejects/waitForActual now appear in stacks since
getAsyncStackTrace uses isHostOrPrivateBuiltinFunction instead of
isHostOrBuiltinFunction.

Apply stackTraceLimit after caller removal, not before

Collecting stackTraceLimit frames from getStackTrace then removing frames
up to the caller could leave an empty trace when the caller is deep.
Collect without a limit, apply Bun's visibility filter and caller removal,
then truncate to stackTraceLimit.

title: Fix per-query memory leaks in MySQL adapter
body:
Fixes #28632

## Problem

The `bun:sql` MySQL adapter leaks memory on every query execution, causing RSS to grow monotonically until OOM on Linux. The JS heap stays stable — the leak is in native Zig allocations.

## Root cause

Three separate leaks, all in the MySQL protocol handling:

1. **`ColumnDefinition41.deinit()` did not free `name_or_index`** — The `name_or_index` field holds a heap-allocated copy of the column name (created via `ColumnIdentifier.init()` → `Data.toOwned()`), but `deinit()` only freed the other `Data` fields (catalog, schema, table, etc.), not `name_or_index`. This leaked on every final cleanup.

2. **`ColumnDefinition41.decodeInternal()` overwrote `name_or_index` without freeing the old value** — When a prepared statement is reused (same column count), the MySQL server re-sends column definitions with each COM_STMT_EXECUTE response. `decodeInternal()` assigned a new `name_or_index` on each re-decode without freeing the previous heap allocation. **This leaked one allocation per column per query execution** — the primary cause of the reported unbounded RSS growth.

3. **`PreparedStatement.Execute.deinit()` didn't free the `params` slice** — The `bind()` function allocates a `[]Value` array for query parameters. `Execute.deinit()` freed each individual `Value` but not the slice itself, leaking the array allocation on every parameterized query.

Also fixed `checkForDuplicateFields()` overwriting `name_or_index` to `.duplicate` without freeing the old owned data.

## Fix

- Add `this.name_or_index.deinit()` to `ColumnDefinition41.deinit()`
- Add `this.name_or_index.deinit()` before reassignment in `decodeInternal()`
- Add `bun.default_allocator.free(this.params)` to `Execute.deinit()`
- Add `field.name_or_index.deinit()` before `.duplicate` assignment in `checkForDuplicateFields()`

## Verification

```
# System bun (unfixed), 5000 queries on 50-column table:
Growth: 17.48 MB

# Debug bun (fixed), same test:
Growth: 6.93 MB
```

`bun bd test test/regression/issue/28632.test.ts` — passes with fix, fails without.
author: robobun

### Level of scrutiny

title: Flush FileSink in process.stdout._final before end() callback fires
body:
## Problem

`process.stdout.end(callback)` fires `callback` before all data has been flushed through the pipe, causing truncation at power-of-2 boundaries (64KB, 128KB, etc.) when the callback calls `process.exit(0)`.

**Repro:**
```js
const output = "x".repeat(200000) + "\n";
process.stdout.write(output);
process.stdout.end(() => { process.exit(0); });
```
```
$ bun repro.js | wc -c
65536          # expected: 200001
```

Node.js outputs all 200001 bytes correctly.

## Root Cause

`process.stdout` uses a fast-path `writeFast()` that bypasses `Writable._writableState` tracking (never updates `pendingcb`). When `end()` is called, `finishMaybe()` sees `pendingcb === 0` and immediately schedules the finish callback — even though the underlying `FileSink` may still have buffered data waiting to be flushed through the pipe.

Additionally, `process.stdout` had no `_final` method defined. The `_final` hook is specifically designed for this purpose: it runs before the stream transitions to "finished" state, giving an opportunity to flush pending data.

## Fix

Add a `_final` method to `process.stdout` and `process.stderr` that calls `sink.flush()` on the underlying `FileSink`. If `flush()` returns a Promise (data still pending in the pipe buffer), the callback waits for it to resolve before the stream finishes.

Closes #25432

Flush FileSink in process.stdout._final before end() callback fires

Add a _final method to process.stdout and process.stderr that flushes
the underlying FileSink before the Writable stream transitions to the

immediately because writeFast bypasses _writableState tracking, so
the stream doesn't know about pending async writes. The callback

boundaries (64KB, 128KB).

title: delete cmake
body:
## What

Removes the CMake build system. The TypeScript build (`scripts/build/`) has been the primary build path for a while; this deletes the legacy path.

## Changes

**Deleted (~6200 lines)**
- `cmake/` — all targets, toolchains, tools, analysis, Sources.json
- `CMakeLists.txt`
- `scripts/build.mjs` — the old cmake wrapper
- `scripts/glob-sources.mjs` — the old Sources.json→txt generator
- 14 package.json scripts that invoked the above (`build:cmake:*`, `analysis*`, `clang-tidy*`, `zig-format*`, `zig:test*`)

**Source patterns: `cmake/Sources.json` → `scripts/glob-sources.ts`**

Patterns are now inlined as a typed const with `Sources` derived via `keyof typeof patterns`. Also runnable as a CLI:

```sh
bun scripts/glob-sources.ts cxx      # one .cpp path per line
bun scripts/glob-sources.ts --all    # every source list
```

Uses `node:fs` `globSync` so it runs under both Bun and Node. Consumers updated: `configure.ts`, `run-clang-format.sh`, `ban-words.test.ts`, `lldb-inline-tool.cpp`, `cppbind.ts`, `sync-webkit-source.ts`.

**New: `bun run clean`**

```sh
bun run clean              # build/debug/
bun run clean release      # build/release/
bun run clean debug-local  # build/debug-local/ + vendor/WebKit/WebKitBuild/Debug
bun run clean zig          # zig caches across all profiles
bun run clean cpp          # obj/ + pch/ across all profiles
bun run clean deep         # build/, vendor/* (except WebKit), zig caches
bun run clean --help
```

The `deep` vendor list is derived from `allDeps` in `scripts/build/deps/index.ts`, so new dependencies are picked up automatically.

**Docs updated**

CONTRIBUTING.md and docs/project/contributing.mdx now point at `scripts/build/deps/webkit.ts` for `WEBKIT_VERSION` and the `--asan=off` flag for disabling ASAN.

## Notes

- `cmake` the tool is still required — vendored deps (zstd, libarchive, boringssl, etc.) build via nested cmake
- `scripts/bump.ts` was already missing (deleted in an earlier commit); the bump action and package.json script that reference it are pre-existing breakage
- `test/js/bun/util/zstd.test.ts` has a snapshot of the old package.json as compression test data; will need a snapshot regen

commits.message:
Remove CMake build system

The TypeScript build system (scripts/build/) is now the only way to build
Bun. This removes cmake/, CMakeLists.txt, scripts/build.mjs, and the
package.json scripts that invoked them.

Source patterns moved from cmake/Sources.json to scripts/glob-sources.ts
(also runnable as a CLI: `bun scripts/glob-sources.ts cxx`). Consumers
updated: configure.ts, run-clang-format.sh, ban-words.test.ts,
lldb-inline-tool.cpp, cppbind.ts.

Also adds `bun run clean` with presets (debug, release, zig, cpp, deep,
etc.) — derives the vendor dep list from allDeps so new dependencies are
picked up automatically.

cmake-the-tool is still required for nested dependency builds (zstd,
libarchive, etc.).
[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes

Bump CI agent Bun to 1.3.1 [build linux images]

fs.globSync (used by scripts/glob-sources.ts) requires Bun >= 1.2.2.
The docker-based Linux agents were pinned to 1.1.38.

- .buildkite/Dockerfile: OLD_BUN_VERSION 1.1.38 -> 1.3.1
- scripts/bootstrap.sh: bump image version 28 -> 29 to invalidate
  cached agent images (getImageName reads this for the tag)

build: DirectBuild deps, ASAN dyld shim, workaround registry, bd quiet mode

DirectBuild: deps simple enough to list files compile as first-class
ninja edges instead of a cmake sub-process. tinycc converted — drops
the overlay CMakeLists.txt and the recurring ASAN workarounds on the
c2str host tool, which now gets -fno-sanitize=all unconditionally.

ASAN dyld shim: macOS 26.4's Dyld.framework reimplemented
dyld_shared_cache_iterate_text in Swift with a _Block_copy that
deadlocks ASAN init (llvm/llvm-project#182943). Shim interposes a
non-allocating version using _dyld_get_dyld_header. Linked via
LC_LOAD_DYLIB + @rpath so it auto-loads with bun-debug — no env var.
Darwin+ASAN only.

workarounds.ts: self-obsoleting registry. Every temporary fix registers
an expectedToBeFixed predicate; configure fails with cleanup
instructions once the upstream fix ships.

build.ts: quiet build output when exec args present (original bd
behavior), `--` separator for flag disambiguation, `--target` accepts
space-separated value, colored target name in done message, signal
exit re-raised. Arg routing documented in file header.

tty.ts: centralized ANSI/TTY abstraction. nameColor() hashes names to
stable colors; stream.ts and the done message share it.

config.ts/tools.ts: clangVersion captured from the existing
toolchain-resolve --version spawn, exposed on Config. findTool returns
{path, version}.

Also:
- configure() no longer prints; build.ts decides based on quiet mode
- provides.sources deps get phonies pointing at compiled .o files
- scripts/bd, scripts/bd.ps1 removed (bd is a package.json script)
- scripts/build/CLAUDE.md: architecture doc with goals, ninja primer,
  common tasks, arg routing
[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes

build: add --quiet flag, enable for bd

Suppresses configure summary and ninja progress; output shown only on
failure. bd passes it so `bun bd` (no args) shows just `[build] done`.
Auto-enabled when exec args are present (existing behavior).

ci: remove link-metadata.json generation

No longer needed. Also fixes Windows CI failure where the script's
`import { $ } from "bun"` couldn't resolve under node.

build: derive TCC_VERSION from commit hash

TCC_VERSION only appears in tinycc's CLI help (not built) and DWARF
producer string — neither load-bearing. Use commit hash so bumping
TINYCC_COMMIT is the only change needed.
[publish linux images]

build: fix DirectBuild codegen tool for cross-compile, fix ci.ts node compat

Codegen tools (tinycc's conftest.c) run on the build host, so they can't
inherit cfg's target/arch flags. Use a bare host-cc rule instead of
cc()/link(). Fixes musl CI "file format not recognized".

Also: ci.ts used import.meta.path (Bun-only) in the annotation fallback
path; use fileURLToPath(import.meta.url) for node compat.
[publish linux images]

ci: install node in Dockerfile for build containers [publish linux images]

Non-musl Linux builds run inside Docker where bootstrap.sh's node
install doesn't apply. Version matches bootstrap.sh (24.3.0).

title: Enable TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT for HTTP listeners on Linux
body:
## What

Sets `TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT` on the listen socket for `Bun.serve()` (Linux) and `SO_ACCEPTFILTER "dataready"` (FreeBSD). When the kernel defers accept, the accept loop dispatches the socket as readable immediately instead of returning to epoll first.

## Why

For short-lived connections (HTTP/1.1 with `Connection: close`), the server currently does:

1. epoll wake → listen socket readable
2. `accept()` → register new socket with epoll
3. **return to epoll** ← wasted round-trip
4. epoll wake → new socket readable
5. `recv()` → process → respond → close

With `TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT`, the kernel holds the accept until data arrives, so step 3–4 collapses into step 2. Same pattern nginx uses ([`rev->ready = 1` after deferred accept](https://github.com/nginx/nginx/blob/master/src/event/ngx_event_accept.c#L258-L263)).

Works for TLS too — the ClientHello is the first data packet.

## Scope

- Opt-in via `LIBUS_LISTEN_DEFER_ACCEPT`, only set by uWS `HttpContext::listen`
- `Bun.listen()` / `net.createServer()` unchanged (may serve server-sends-first protocols)
- No-op on macOS (no kernel equivalent) and Windows

## Test plan

- [x] `test/js/bun/http/serve.test.ts` passes (189/189)
- [x] `zig:check-all` passes on all platforms
- [ ] Benchmark `oha --disable-keepalive` on Linux to quantify improvement
author: Jarred-Sumner

<details>
<summary>Extended reasoning...</summary>

### Overview
The PR adds TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT (Linux) / SO_ACCEPTFILTER (FreeBSD) to all HTTP listen sockets via a new LIBUS_LISTEN_DEFER_ACCEPT flag. Changes span: a new bsd_set_defer_accept() helper in bsd.c, a deferred_accept field added to us_listen_socket_t, flag propagation in context.c, and an immediate recursive call to us_internal_dispatch_ready_poll() inside the accept loop in loop.c. HttpContext::listen unconditionally OR-s the new flag, affecting every Bun.serve() HTTP/HTTPS server.

### Security risks
No direct security risks. TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT with a 1-second timeout (matching nginx) is a well-understood kernel feature. The timeout prevents SYN-flood half-open connections from occupying queue slots indefinitely. No auth, crypto, or permission surface is touched.

### Level of scrutiny
High scrutiny is warranted. This is not a config or comment change — it changes the accept-loop control flow for every HTTP server in Bun. Key concerns: (1) The recursive us_internal_dispatch_ready_poll() call from within the POLL_TYPE_SEMI_SOCKET branch adds a new re-entrancy pattern that needs careful review. (2) The SSL code path (us_internal_ssl_socket_context_listen) is not shown in the diff; it is unclear whether the deferred_accept flag is correctly propagated to the underlying listen socket for TLS servers. (3) The interaction with the low-priority SSL handshake queue and socket adoption (flags.adopted / s->prev) after the deferred dispatch adds complexity. (4) The benchmark (oha --disable-keepalive) is explicitly listed as not yet done in the test plan.

### Other factors
No bugs were found by the automated system, and the 189/189 serve.test.ts pass rate is rea

This PR bundles two unrelated changes — a TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT networking optimization and a JSC GC-assertion fix — both of which touch critical, production-path code. A human reviewer should validate the recursive `us_internal_dispatch_ready_poll` call in the accept loop and the `hasMaterializedErrorInfo` branch in `errorConstructorFuncCaptureStackTrace` before merging.


<details>
<summary>Extended reasoning...</summary>

### Overview
The PR modifies eight files across two independent concerns: (1) enabling TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT / SO_ACCEPTFILTER on HTTP listen sockets to skip an unnecessary epoll round-trip on accept, and (2) fixing a JSC GC assertion that fires when Error.captureStackTrace is called on an already-materialized ErrorInstance.

### Security risks
No apparent security risks. TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT is opt-in and scoped exclusively to HttpContext::listen; Bun.listen() and net.createServer() are not affected. The flag is gated by successful setsockopt() return, so a kernel that does not support it degrades gracefully. The JSC change is internal error bookkeeping with no trust-boundary implications.

### Level of scrutiny
Both changes warrant careful human review despite no bugs being found:

**Networking (loop.c):** After on_open fires, the code now makes a *recursive* call to `us_internal_dispatch_ready_poll` with LIBUS_SOCKET_READABLE. This re-enters the same dispatch function under a different poll type (POLL_TYPE_SOCKET vs POLL_TYPE_SEMI_SOCKET), which is architecturally unusual. The comment acknowledges EWOULDBLOCK as a rare-but-possible outcome (defer timeout). Under high connection rates, the stack depth of this recursion needs human validation.

**JSC (FormatStackTraceForJS.cpp):** The fix introduces a new branch for `hasMaterializedErrorInfo()` that eagerly computes and sets the stack property. This path bypasses the lazy-getter mechanism that the rest of the error-stack machinery relies on. While the logic appears correct for preventing the GC assertion, JS

commits.message:
Enable TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT for HTTP listeners on Linux

For short-lived HTTP/1.1 connections (Connection: close), the server
currently has to return to epoll after accept() just to learn the
request is readable. With TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT, the kernel holds the accept
until the client has actually sent data, so the request (or ClientHello
for TLS) is guaranteed to be in the buffer when accept() returns.

The accept loop now dispatches readable immediately for sockets from a
deferred listener, skipping one epoll round-trip per connection. This is
the same pattern nginx uses (rev->ready = 1 after deferred accept).

The option is opt-in via LIBUS_LISTEN_DEFER_ACCEPT and only set by the
uWS HTTP listener — raw TCP via Bun.listen/net.createServer is unchanged
since those may serve protocols where the server sends first.

SO_ACCEPTFILTER "dataready" is set on FreeBSD for the same effect. No-op
on macOS and Windows.

Fix m_errorInfoMaterialized assertion in GC after captureStackTrace

Error.captureStackTrace called setStackFrames() after
materializeErrorInfoIfNeeded(), leaving m_errorInfoMaterialized=true
with a non-null m_stackTrace. When GC's finalizeUnconditionally found
unmarked frames it called computeErrorInfo() which asserts
!m_errorInfoMaterialized, causing SIGILL under ASAN.

When error info is already materialized, eagerly compute and set the
.stack property instead of storing new frames via setStackFrames().

title: Add second cork buffer to uWebSockets for nested async handlers
body:
## What

Replaces uWebSockets' single shared cork buffer with two independent cork slots.

## Why

When an async HTTP handler awaits and later resumes (e.g. another request resolves its promise), the resumed request's `writeHead()` + `end()` couldn't get the cork buffer because the newer in-flight request was holding it. This forced the resumed request down the uncorked path — one `write()` syscall per call instead of batching them. On the `bench/snippets/http-hello.node.mjs` interleaved-await pattern this dropped throughput from ~190k req/s to ~22k req/s.

## How

- **Two independent slots**: each holds `{buffer, socket, offset, ssl}`. `cork()` grabs whichever slot is free; `uncork()` releases yours. No ordering constraints.
- **Empty-slot stealing**: if both slots are claimed but one has `offset==0` (corked but not yet written), it's freely stolen — no flush needed.
- **LRU eviction**: when both slots hold actual data (rare, depth 3+), force-uncork the least-recently-touched one. Tracked via a single bit.
- **Entry-point re-cork**: `NodeHTTP writeHead` and `HttpResponse::cork(handler)` call `cork()` before each write sequence, so if your slot was stolen while you were awaiting, you transparently get a fresh one.
- **UAF protection**: `uncorkWithoutSending()` (called from all close/destroy paths) now clears the socket from any slot via `unborrowCorkSlot()`, preventing the drain loop from dereferencing a freed pointer.
- **Bounded drain**: `preCb` and `uws_res_clear_corked_socket` iterate at most twice (one per slot).

Memory cost: one extra 16KB buffer per event loop.

## Tests

- `test/js/node/http/node-http-nested-cork.test.ts` — 20 concurrent interleaved-await requests for both node:http and Bun.serve, asserts all responses are correct with no cross-socket bleed.
- `serve.test.ts` (189 tests), `node-http.test.ts` (78 tests), `websocket-server.test.ts` all pass.
author: Jarred-Sumner

This is a well-described fix for a real throughput regression, but it's a non-trivial change to core networking infrastructure that warrants a human look before merging.


<details>
<summary>Extended reasoning...</summary>

### Overview
The PR replaces the single shared uWebSockets cork buffer with two independent cork slots (each 16KB), touching 7 C++ files across the low-level HTTP/WebSocket send path: LoopData.h (data model), AsyncSocket.h (cork/uncork/getSendBuffer/write), HttpResponse.h (upgrade path + cork handler), Loop.h and libuwsockets.cpp (drain loops), WebSocket.h (large-message fast path), and NodeHTTP.cpp (writeHead).

### Security risks
No direct auth or crypto changes. The UAF protection fix (unborrowCorkSlot called from all close/destroy paths) is a positive security improvement — the old single-slot code left a dangling pointer risk if a socket was freed while corked. The new code explicitly clears both slots on destruction.

### Level of scrutiny
High. This is production-critical code that runs on every HTTP and WebSocket send. The LRU eviction path in AsyncSocket::cork() has a subtle edge case: if a depth-3+ nesting causes LRU eviction of the slot captured by HttpResponse::cork() before handler() returns, the post-handler uncork could operate on the wrong slot. The PR description acknowledges depth-3+ is rare, but the correctness argument for that case deserves explicit human verification. The drain loops (preCb, uws_res_clear_corked_socket) both iterate exactly twice, which is bounded correctly for two slots.

### Other factors
- The only bug found is a trivial unused import (normalizeBunSnapshot) in the new test file, which has no runtime impact.
- New tests cover the core scenario (20 concurrent interleaved-await requests) for both node:http and Bun.serve.
- Existing test suites (serve.test.ts, node-http.test.ts, websocket-server.test.ts) reportedly pass.
- Memory cost is one additional 16KB buffer per event loop, which is negligible.

</detai

commits.message:
Add second cork buffer to uWebSockets for nested async handlers

When an awaited HTTP handler resumes while another request holds the single
cork buffer, the resumed request's writeHead+end were forced down the
uncorked path (one syscall per write). With two independent cork slots,
both the in-flight request and the resumed request can batch their writes.

Slots are borrowed on cork(), stolen if empty (offset==0), and the LRU
slot is force-flushed only when both hold data. Close paths clear slots
to avoid dangling pointers in the drain loop.
[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes

Remove canCork() guards before cork() calls

The canCork() guards prevented cork() from ever reaching its LRU
force-uncork fallback — if both slots were full, we'd skip corking
entirely instead of evicting. cork() now always makes progress, so
callers just check !isCorked().

Also: use wtf/Assertions.h ASSERT instead of custom UWS_CORK_ASSERT,
and only set needsUncork in App.h if cork() actually succeeded.
Remove dead AsyncSocket::canCork() wrapper

Strengthen cork buffer tests to catch cross-socket data bleed

9 test variants covering node:http and Bun.serve (buffered, pull stream,
type:"direct", async generator) with multi-chunk writes and yields
between chunks. Each response validated byte-for-byte so any foreign
data from another request's cork slot fails the exact match.

Rewrite cork tests as adversarial data-bleed attacks

10 attack vectors designed to trigger cross-socket data leakage:
write-yield-write with slot held, yield-write with empty slot stolen,
rapid microtask churn, burst-flush-burst, large chunks at buffer
boundary. Covers node:http, Bun.serve buffered, type:direct, and async
generator. 32 concurrent requests x 8 chunks each, byte-exact match.

Fix cork slot handling in TopicTree lambda and cork(handler)

App.h: reset needsUncork at FIRST and after LAST in the pub/sub lambda.
The mutable capture persisted across subscriber iterations, so if
subscriber B was already corked (FIRST block skipped), it inherited A's
needsUncork=true and got wrongly uncorked.

HttpResponse.h: in cork(handler), check if we're still in a slot after
handler() returns instead of reading whatever socket is in the slot we
originally took. If our empty slot was stolen during an await, the old
code would uncork the thief and return it cast to HttpResponse. Now we
just return — our data (if any) was already flushed by whoever evicted
us, and the upgrade case is handled by HttpContext or the drain loop.

Keep cork alive across WebSocket upgrade

internalEnd was uncorking before the upgrade path captured the cork
slot, so the handshake went out in its own syscall and the WebSocket
started uncorked. Now internalEnd takes a keepCorked flag that the
upgrade path sets, letting the handshake batch with the first frames
written in the open handler.

The slot briefly holds a dangling old-this pointer between adopt_socket
and transferCorkSlot, but nothing dereferences it in that window.
Allow exact-fill writes into cork buffer in getSendBuffer

title: fix(serve): leak when Promise<Response> never settles after abort
body:
## Summary

When `Bun.serve()`'s fetch handler returns a pending `Promise<Response>` and the client disconnects before it settles, the `RequestContext` leaked forever. The `ctx.ref()` before `.then()` was only balanced by `onResolve`/`onReject`, which never fire for unsettled promises.

This introduces `NativePromiseContext` — a minimal GC-managed JSCell that owns the ref:

- **`JSC::JSCell`** + `CellType` + custom `IsoHeapCellType` for destructor support
- **`CompactPointerTuple<Pointee*, Tag>`** packs pointer + tag in 8 bytes (cell = JSCell header + 1 word)
- **Tag enum class** with one entry per concrete type (4 RequestContext variants), kept in sync C++↔Zig
- **`take()`** transfers ownership for deterministic cleanup in the normal path
- **Destructor** calls one extern C function that Zig implements with a tag switch — no function pointers, no `Weak<>` overhead

If the Promise settles: `onResolve` calls `take()` → processes → `deref()`. Cell destructor later sees null, no-ops.

If the Promise is GC'd without settling: cell collected with its reaction → destructor → `deref()`. No leak, no UAF.

Applied to all three `.then()` callsites in `RequestContext`: main response promise, error handler promise, and response body stream promise.

## Test plan

- [x] New test: `test/js/bun/http/serve-pending-promise-abort-leak.test.ts` — 4 tests covering the leak fix, normal resolution, resolve-in-abort-handler, and resolve-after-abort-via-setTimeout (UAF safety)
- [x] Test fails on system Bun: `LEAK: 100 RequestContexts were never freed`
- [x] Test passes with fix: `pendingRequests: 0`
- [x] `heapStats().objectTypeCounts.NativePromiseContext` shows 50 for 50 concurrent pending, 0 after resolve+GC
- [x] `serve.test.ts`: 189 pass
- [x] `bun-serve-routes.test.ts` + `bun-serve-headers.test.ts`: 41 pass
- [x] `BUN_JSC_validateExceptionChecks=1`: clean
author: Jarred-Sumner

commits.message:
fix(serve): leak when Promise<Response> never settles after client abort
When the fetch handler returns a pending Promise<Response> and the client
disconnects before it settles, the RequestContext leaked forever. The
ctx.ref() before .then() was only balanced by onResolve/onReject, which
never fire for unsettled promises.

Introduces NativePromiseContext: a minimal JSCell (CellType + custom
IsoHeapCellType) that owns the ref. The cell is passed to .then() instead
of a raw pointer. If the Promise settles, onResolve/onReject call take()
to transfer ownership and deref deterministically. If the Promise is GC'd
without settling, the cell is collected with its reaction and the
destructor releases the ref.

The cell packs the pointer and type tag into a single CompactPointerTuple
(8 bytes of storage beyond the JSCell header). The tag dispatches to the
right deref() via a hardcoded switch in Zig — no function pointers, no
Weak<> overhead.

Applied to all three .then() callsites in RequestContext: the main
response promise, the error handler promise, and the response body stream
promise.

fix(serve): defer NativePromiseContext deref out of GC sweep

The JSCell destructor runs during GC sweep where touching the JSC heap
is unsafe. RequestContext.deref() can cascade into deinit() which
detaches responses, unrefs bodies, and calls server.onRequestComplete()
— all of which may unprotect JS values. This tripped "Fatal: collector
does not believe that the world is stopped" on Linux CI.

Enqueue a DeferredDerefTask instead and run the deref on the next
event-loop tick. Zero-allocation: the ctx pointer and Tag pack into the
task's u49 _ptr slot (pointer in bits 3..48, tag in bits 0..2 — target
types are all >= 8-byte aligned), leaving the u15 Task discriminant
intact. Same trick PosixSignalTask uses for signal numbers.

title: feat(cron): add Bun.cron API for OS-level cron jobs and expression parsing
body:
## Summary

Adds a complete cron API to Bun: register OS-level cron jobs, remove them, and parse cron expressions to compute the next matching time.

### New APIs

```ts
// Register a cron job (uses crontab on Linux, launchd on macOS, Task Scheduler on Windows)
await Bun.cron("./worker.ts", "30 2 * * MON", "weekly-report");

// Parse a cron expression → next matching UTC Date
const next = Bun.cron.parse("*/15 * * * *");
const weekday = Bun.cron.parse("0 9 * * MON-FRI");
const jan1 = Bun.cron.parse("@yearly");

// Remove a registered job
await Bun.cron.remove("weekly-report");
```

### How a cron job runs

When registered, the OS scheduler invokes `bun run --cron-title=<title> --cron-period='<schedule>' <script>` at each scheduled time. Bun imports the module, finds `default.scheduled()`, and calls it:

```ts
// worker.ts — the script registered with Bun.cron()
export default {
  async scheduled(controller) {
    // controller.type === "scheduled"
    // controller.cron === "30 2 * * 1"       (the normalized schedule)
    // controller.scheduledTime === 1737340200000  (ms since epoch)

    await doWork();
  },
};
```

This follows the [Cloudflare Workers Cron Triggers API](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/handlers/scheduled/). The handler can be `async` — Bun waits for the promise to settle before exiting. `--cron-title` and `--cron-period` must be provided together (validated at startup).

### Cron expression features

- **Standard 5-field format**: `minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`
- **Named days**: `SUN`–`SAT`, `Sunday`–`Saturday` (case-insensitive)
- **Named months**: `JAN`–`DEC`, `January`–`December` (case-insensitive)
- **Sunday as 7**: weekday field accepts `7` as alias for `0`
- **Nicknames**: `@yearly`, `@annually`, `@monthly`, `@weekly`, `@daily`, `@midnight`, `@hourly`
- **POSIX OR logic**: when both day-of-month and day-of-week are specified (neither is `*`), either matching fires the job — standard [POSIX cron](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/utilities/crontab.html) behavior
- **All operators**: `*`, `,` (list), `-` (range), `/` (step)
- Named values and nicknames are normalized to numeric form before registration

### How `Bun.cron()` works per platform

| Platform | Backend | Logs |
|----------|---------|------|
| Linux | [crontab](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/crontab.5.html) | `journalctl -u cron` |
| macOS | [launchd](https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/MacOSX/Conceptual/BPSystemStartup/Chapters/CreatingLaunchdJobs.html) plist with cartesian-product `StartCalendarInterval` | `/tmp/bun.cron.<title>.{stdout,stderr}.log` |
| Windows | [schtasks](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/taskschd/task-scheduler-start-page) with `/st` start time and `/d` day | Event Viewer → TaskScheduler |

Re-registering with the same title overwrites the existing job in-place.

### Implementation details

- **Parser** (`cron_parser.zig`): Bitset representation — `u64` for minutes, `u32` for hours/days, `u16` for months, `u8` for weekdays. Uses `bun.ComptimeStringMap` with `getASCIIICaseInsensitive()` for name lookups and `bun.strings` throughout.
- **Next-occurrence algorithm**: Advances by largest non-matching unit (month → day → hour → minute), normalizes via JSC's UTC `GregorianDateTime` functions each iteration. Searches up to ~4 years.
- **C++ bindings**: Added `Bun__gregorianDateTimeToMSUTC` and `Bun__msToGregorianDateTimeUTC` for UTC time decomposition/recomposition.
- **Schedule normalization**: Named values (`Monday`, `@daily`) are converted to numeric form (`1`, `0 0 * * *`) via `formatNumeric()` before platform registration.
- **Path resolution**: Uses `vm.transpiler.resolver.resolve()` relative to the caller's source directory for full module resolution (TypeScript, package.json exports, node_modules).
- **macOS `cronToCalendarInterval`**: Now expands comma-separated values into cartesian-product `<dict>` entries, supporting complex expressions.
- **Windows `cronToSchtasks`**: Now passes `/st HH:MM` start time and `/d DAY` for DAILY/WEEKLY schedules.
- **`--cron-title`/`--cron-period` validation**: Must be provided together (fails fast at startup otherwise).
- **`bun.sys.getcwd`**: Uses bun's syscall wrapper instead of `std.posix.getcwd`.
- **`bun.env_var`**: Uses type-safe cached env var accessors (`HOME`, `PATH`) instead of `bun.getenvZ`.

### Bug fixes

- **`filterCrontab` substring matching**: Fixed pre-existing bug where removing job `"test"` could also remove `"test-cleanup"` due to `indexOf` substring matching. Changed to exact line match with `bun.strings.eql`.

## Test plan

- [x] 61 tests, 161 assertions pass (`bun bd test test/js/bun/cron/cron.test.ts`)
- [x] Cross-platform compilation: 61/61 targets (`bun run zig:check-all`)
- [x] Tests fail with system bun (verifying they test new functionality)
- [x] Registration/removal tests gated with `describe.skipIf(!isLinux)` and save/restore original crontab
- [x] Sequential `parse()` calls verify patterns produce correct sequences (not just single results)
- [x] OR logic explicitly tested: `0 0 15 * FRI` produces both 15th-of-month and Friday matches, with assertion that Jan 15 (Wednesday) appears in results
- [x] All 7 day names and 12 month names verified via `getUTCDay()`/`getUTCMonth()`
- [x] `MON-FRI/2` verified to produce Mon, Wed, Fri sequence
- [x] Nicknames verified against equivalent 5-field expressions over multiple results
- [x] Edge cases: leap year Feb 29, day-31 month skipping, impossible dates return null, year boundary crossing, NaN/Infinity rejection

## Review — `Bun.cron` API

### Code quality ✅

The implementation is clean and well-structured across all 16 files:

**Parser (`cron_parser.zig`)** — Correct bitset-based 5-field cron parser with proper POSIX OR semantics for day-of-month/day-of-week. Named days/months, nicknames, steps, ranges all handled. The `next()` function uses JSC's gregorian date conversion for correct calendar math (leap years, month lengths, weekday computation) with a bounded iteration limit (~4 years).

**Registration (`cron.zig`)** — Async, event-loop-integrated implementation using `bun.spawn`:
- Linux: reads existing crontab → filters out old entry → appends new → installs via temp file. Correct state machine.
- macOS: writes launchd plist with XML-escaped values → bootout old → bootstrap new. Properly handles POSIX OR semantics by splitting into separate `<dict>` entries in `StartCalendarInterval`.
- Windows: maps cron expressions to `schtasks` params. Correctly limited to simple patterns.

**Security:** Title validation (`[a-zA-Z0-9_-]`), single-quote shell escaping for crontab paths, XML escaping for plist values, `O_EXCL` for temp files, JS string escaping for the eval wrapper.

**Execution mode (`bun.js.zig`)** — Clean eval-source injection: imports the module, calls `default.scheduled(controller)`, awaits the promise. Follows Cloudflare Workers Cron Triggers API.

**Types (`bun.d.ts`)** — `CronWithAutocomplete` type provides IDE autocomplete for common expressions while accepting arbitrary strings. Return types correct.

**Tests (`cron.test.ts`)** — 800+ lines covering: API shape, registration/removal (Linux only, gated on `hasCrontab`), execution mode via subprocess, and thorough parse tests including POSIX OR logic, named days/months, nicknames, boundary cases, and error cases.

## CI Gate: FAIL

Build #39586 completed with 5 Windows job failures. All failures are in `test/js/bun/cron/cron.test.ts` — `ENOENT` when spawning `schtasks`.

### Root cause

`src/bun.js/api/cron.zig` (lines ~520 and ~769) passes bare `"schtasks"` as `argv[0]` to `bun.spawn.spawnProcess`. The Zig-level spawn doesn't do PATH resolution on Windows, so the process fails with ENOENT.

The JS-level `hasSchtasks` guard works fine because `Bun.spawnSync` does PATH resolution — but the actual register/unregister calls go through the Zig spawn path which doesn't.

### Fix needed

Resolve `schtasks` to its full path before spawning. Options:
1. Use `bun.which` to resolve `schtasks` → full path, then use that in argv[0]
2. Hardcode `C:\Windows\System32\schtasks.exe` (less robust)  
3. Spawn via `cmd.exe /C schtasks ...` (PATH resolution happens via cmd)

Option 1 is cleanest — store the resolved path from the `hasSchtasks` check and reuse it.

- darwin `double-connect.test.ts` snapshot failure is a pre-existing flake (confirmed fails on baked main binary too)

  - `upload-benchmark.mjs` — infra flake (also fails on main `10bdb48`).

commits.message:
feat(cron): add Bun.cron API for OS-level cron jobs and expression parsing

Adds `Bun.cron()`, `Bun.cron.remove()`, and `Bun.cron.parse()` — a
complete API for registering OS-level cron jobs and parsing cron
expressions from JavaScript.

**Bun.cron(path, schedule, title)** registers a cron job that runs a
JS/TS module on a schedule, using the platform's native scheduler:
- Linux: crontab
- macOS: launchd (plist + StartCalendarInterval)
- Windows: Task Scheduler (schtasks)

The target module exports a `scheduled(controller)` handler following
the Cloudflare Workers Cron Triggers API.

**Bun.cron.parse(expression, relativeDate?)** parses a cron expression
and returns the next matching UTC Date. Supports:
- Standard 5-field expressions (minute hour day month weekday)
- Named days: SUN-SAT, Sunday-Saturday (case-insensitive)
- Named months: JAN-DEC, January-December (case-insensitive)
- Sunday as 7 (normalized to 0)
- Predefined nicknames: @yearly, @annually, @monthly, @weekly, @daily,
  @midnight, @hourly
- POSIX OR logic: when both day-of-month and day-of-week are specified
  (neither is *), either matching is sufficient

The parser uses a bitset representation (u64/u32/u16/u8 per field) for
efficient matching. The next-occurrence algorithm advances by the
largest non-matching unit (month > day > hour > minute) and normalizes
via JSC's UTC date functions on each iteration.

Expressions are normalized to numeric form before platform registration,
so named values like "Monday" or "@daily" produce valid crontab entries.

Also fixes a pre-existing bug in filterCrontab where substring matching
(indexOf) could cause removing job "test" to also remove "test-cleanup".
Changed to exact line matching.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes
implement a CronWithAutocomplete

Use disposable pattern for crontab state and detect crontab via PATH

- Replace beforeEach/afterEach with `using _restore = saveCrontabState()`
  so crontab is always restored even if a test crashes
- Use Bun.which("crontab") instead of hardcoded /usr/bin/crontab
- Skip cron tests based on crontab availability, not just isLinux

fix: address review feedback on cron implementation

- Build schtasks argv dynamically to avoid null gaps truncating before /f
- Initialize windows.loop in SpawnOptions for Windows spawn support
- Wrap multiple calendar interval dicts in <array> for valid launchd plist
- Use minute value for /st in HOURLY schtasks schedule
- Resolve named weekdays (MON, TUE, etc.) in cronToSchtasks
- Use bun.path.dirname instead of std.fs.path.dirname in resolvePath
- Fix JSDoc chaining example to handle null return
- Add common cron patterns and template literal to CronWithAutocomplete type
- Reject empty --cron-title/--cron-period values in argument validation

https://claude.ai/code/session_01YZgjSQgUz2Xt7SZKNHv5jv

Fix all 11 review issues on cron implementation

cron.zig:
1. Use this.schedule (not raw_schedule) in cronToSchtasks so nicknames
   like @daily get the normalized 5-field form
2. Accept dow=7 as Sunday (POSIX cron allows both 0 and 7)
3. Use bun.strings.eql instead of std.mem.eql in resolveWeekdayName
4. Use bun.String.static instead of jsc.ZigString.static in getCronObject
5. Create ~/Library/LaunchAgents dir before writing plist file
6. Fix buffer overflow in MINUTE case of cronToSchtasks — use
   std.fmt.bufPrintZ instead of raw @memcpy of user input
7. Exempt Windows schtasks /delete non-zero exit in CronRemoveJob so
   removing a non-existent task resolves without error
8. Use std.c.environ for env inheritance instead of null pointer that
   gave child processes an empty environment
9. Implement POSIX cron OR semantics for launchd: when both day-of-month
   and day-of-week are specified, emit separate dict sets so launchd
   fires when either matches

docs/runtime/cron.mdx:
10. Fix grep pattern: bun.cron not sh.bun.cron
11. Document that complex cron patterns are supported on macOS via
    Cartesian product expansion

Fix ban-words and macOS CI failures

- Replace std.fs.cwd() with bun.FD.cwd().makePath() in cron.zig
- Skip crontab-based registration/removal tests on macOS (cron uses
  launchd on macOS, not crontab, so verifying via crontab is wrong)

Fix Windows compilation: guard std.c.environ with isPosix

std.c.environ is not available on Windows since Bun doesn't link libc
there. Use undefined for the Windows branch since cron is POSIX-only
(crontab on Linux, launchd on macOS).

Fix Windows envp crash in spawnCmdGeneric and rebase on main

The spawnCmdGeneric function set envp to `undefined` on Windows,
causing undefined behavior when passed to spawnProcess/libuv.
Use zeroed pointer instead, which libuv interprets as "inherit
parent environment".

Also rebased on current main to pick up recent fixes for
darwin-13 and Windows test stability.

Fix Windows zig build: use unreachable for POSIX-only cron envp

std.mem.zeroes cannot zero-initialize a many-item pointer type.
Since cron is POSIX-only, use unreachable in the else branch.

Revert "Fix Windows zig build: use unreachable for POSIX-only cron envp"

This reverts commit 97751444a55d549e4555b973b5202f19745db955.

Fix Windows zig build: use empty sentinel-terminated array for cron envp

std.mem.zeroes can't zero-initialize a sentinel-terminated pointer type
on Windows. Use a pointer to an empty null-terminated array literal
instead, which is a valid value for the [*:null]?[*:0]const u8 type.

Fix Windows cron: use cross-platform envp instead of unreachable

Replace unreachable in spawnCmdGeneric's Windows envp branch with
createNullDelimitedEnvMap, which builds a proper environment block
from the VM's env map. Add Windows-specific schtasks registration
and removal tests.

Add macOS launchd tests for cron registration and removal

Verify plist creation, CalendarInterval XML content, launchctl
bootstrap/bootout lifecycle, and cleanup on removal.

Expand Windows schtasks cron tests for parity with Linux/macOS

Add content verification (cron flags, schedule type), multi-task
registration, targeted removal, and re-registration tests.
Add missing macOS cron tests for multi-job, targeted removal, and re-registration

Add cross-platform cron API consistency tests and Windows limitation tests

Test that @daily, @weekly, @hourly, named weekdays, and paths with
spaces all register successfully on every platform. Explicitly test
that Windows rejects expressions schtasks cannot represent (@monthly,
@yearly, ranges, day-of-month). Add macOS tests for named day
normalization and @daily CalendarInterval content.

Skip cross-platform cron tests when no cron backend is available

CI runners may not have crontab/launchctl/schtasks available.
Add hasAnyCronBackend guard to the cross-platform API consistency
describe block, matching the pattern used by platform-specific tests.

Probe launchctl GUI domain before running macOS cron tests

Headless CI runners lack a GUI session, so launchctl bootstrap/bootout
gui/<uid> fails. Probe with `launchctl print gui/<uid>` at test setup
and skip macOS-specific cron tests when the domain is unavailable.

Replace schtasks CLI flags with XML-based task registration (#28082)

Switches Windows cron registration from schtasks CLI flags to XML-based
task definitions, enabling full cron expression support on Windows.

**Changes:**
- New `cronToTaskXml()` builds Task Scheduler 2.0 XML with
CalendarTrigger elements from cron bitsets
- `startWindows()` writes XML to temp file, registers via `schtasks
/create /xml`
- `makeTempPath` uses `%TEMP%` on Windows
- Stores `parsed_cron` on CronRegisterJob for XML generation

**Tests:**
- Former "Windows limitations" tests (`@monthly`, `@yearly`, ranges,
day-of-month) now verify successful registration
- All existing tests unchanged

Fix Windows CI: use full schtasks.exe path, probe availability, better errors

- Use C:\Windows\System32\schtasks.exe instead of bare "schtasks"
  to avoid PATH resolution issues on Windows CI
- Probe schtasks availability before running Windows tests (like
  the launchctl probe for macOS)
- Include error details in spawn failure messages for debugging

Fix integer overflow in trigger count and Windows spawn failures

- Widen @popCount results from u7/u6 to u32 before multiplying
  (60 * 24 = 1440 overflows u7 max of 127, causing panic)
- Use full schtasks.exe path on Windows
- Probe schtasks availability before running Windows tests
- Include error details in spawn failure messages

Revert full schtasks.exe path — use bare name for PATH resolution

The full C:\Windows\System32 path caused ENOENT on baseline CI
runners. Bare "schtasks" works via libuv's CreateProcessW which
searches PATH. The hasSchtasks probe already verifies this works.
Retry CI
Retry CI (infra flakes on CPP build steps)

Fix Windows ENOENT: resolve executable via bun.which before spawn

libuv on Windows doesn't do PATH+PATHEXT resolution for bare names
like "schtasks". Bun.spawn (JS API) resolves via bun.which first,
but our Zig code was passing bare names directly to spawnProcess.
Resolve argv[0] through bun.which on Windows, matching Bun.spawn.
Retry CI: windows-2019-x64-baseline-test flake

Heap-copy bun.which result to avoid stack pointer issues on Windows

bun.which returns a pointer into a stack-local PathBuffer. Copy the
result to heap before passing as argv0, matching the pattern used by
Bun.spawn in js_bun_spawn_bindings.zig. Also fix the defer to free
the heap copy after spawn completes.

Fix Windows ENOENT: set CWD on spawn options

SpawnOptions.cwd defaults to "" (empty string). On Windows, libuv
treats a non-NULL empty cwd as an explicit empty directory instead
of inheriting the current directory via GetCurrentDirectoryW. This
causes search_path to fail with ENOENT since it can't resolve the
executable relative to an empty base path.

Every other spawn callsite in Bun (Bun.spawn, lifecycle scripts,
shell) sets .cwd explicitly. Set it to the VM's top_level_dir.

Also simplify bun.which to use stack buffer directly (the spawn
is synchronous so the buffer outlives the libuv call).

Add Windows schtasks diagnostic test to capture XML errors

schtasks /create /xml fails with exit code 1 on CI. The error
message is generic — add a diagnostic test that writes known-good
XML and captures schtasks stderr to identify whether the issue is
XML format, permissions, or service availability.

Also revert probe to query-based (the issue is XML, not perms).

Fix Windows XML encoding: remove encoding attribute from declaration

schtasks uses MSXML which fails to switch encoding mid-parse when
it encounters encoding="UTF-8" — error "(1,40): unable to switch
the encoding". Remove the explicit encoding attribute; XML without
an encoding attribute defaults to UTF-8 per spec, and our content
is pure ASCII so it works in any single-byte encoding.

Capture stderr from spawned tools on Windows for better errors

When schtasks or other tools fail, the user previously saw only
"Process exited with code 1" with no explanation. Now on Windows,
stderr is captured via a BufferedReader (matching the pattern from
lifecycle_script_runner.zig) and included in the error message.

For example, an XML encoding issue now shows:
  "ERROR: The task XML is malformed. (1,40)::ERROR: unable to
   switch the encoding"
instead of the opaque "Process exited with code 1".

Fix Windows SID error: add Principals section to task XML

schtasks fails with "No mapping between account names and security
IDs was done" when XML has no <Principals> section — it tries to
auto-resolve the current user but fails on CI service accounts.

Add explicit <Principal> with InteractiveToken logon type and
LeastPrivilege run level, which tells Task Scheduler to run the
task as whoever is interactively logged in, without needing to
resolve a specific username to a SID.

Use S4U logon type instead of InteractiveToken for Windows tasks

InteractiveToken requires an interactive login session — tasks
silently never fire on headless servers or CI. S4U (Service-for-
User) runs as the registering user without needing a login session,
matching Linux crontab behavior where jobs fire regardless of
whether anyone is logged in. No password is stored.

Trade-off: S4U tasks cannot access network resources, but running
bun scripts locally doesn't need network auth tokens.

Docs: document the S4U logon type and network access limitation.

Clarify S4U network limitation in docs

TCP/IP networking (fetch, HTTP, WebSocket, databases) works fine
under S4U. Only Windows-authenticated resources (SMB shares,
mapped drives, Kerberos/NTLM) are unavailable.

Fix Windows SID error: remove Principals from XML, use /np CLI flag

When XML contains <LogonType> without <UserId>, schtasks attempts
a name-to-SID lookup for the current user which fails on CI service
accounts (error 0x80070534). Remove the Principals section entirely
and let /np handle S4U logon via CLI-side token resolution.

/np without /ru uses the current process's security token directly,
avoiding any username-to-SID lookup. On developer desktops this is
the logged-in user; on CI (SYSTEM) it resolves to S-1-5-18.
Add whoami diagnostic to identify CI service account

Probe schtasks by actually creating a task, not just querying

schtasks /query works on CI service accounts that can't resolve
their identity to a SID, but /create fails. The probe now tries
to actually create and delete a test task, so Windows cron tests
are skipped on CI machines where task creation is fundamentally
unsupported (service accounts without resolvable SIDs).

Probe schtasks via XML path to match what Bun.cron actually uses

The previous probe used CLI flags (/sc ONCE) which work on CI
service accounts, but Bun.cron uses XML import (/xml) which
triggers SID resolution. The probe now tests the same XML path
so tests skip on environments where XML import fails.

Remove diagnostic tests (served their debugging purpose).
Gate XML scheduling tests on hasSchtasks, not isWindows

Fall back to SYSTEM account when SID resolution fails on Windows

On headless servers and CI, the service account's username can't
be resolved to a SID, causing schtasks /create /np to fail. Now
detects the "No mapping between account names and security IDs"
error in stderr and automatically retries with /ru SYSTEM.

- Desktop users: /np works, task runs as current user
- Headless servers/CI: /np fails, retries with /ru SYSTEM,
  task runs as SYSTEM (appropriate for headless environments)

Test probe also tries both paths so tests run on CI.

Add Windows trigger-limit tests and document platform limitations

Tests:
- Verify non-divisor minute steps (*/7, */8, */9, */11, */13) throw
  on Windows with "too many triggers" error but succeed on Linux/macOS
- Verify restricted-field expressions that prevent Repetition
  optimization also throw on Windows (*/15 with month, 6×9 combos)
- Verify POSIX OR-split expressions (0,30 * 15 * 5) exceed limit
  on Windows (2×24×2=96 triggers) but succeed on other platforms

Docs:
- Document S4U user context and SYSTEM fallback for headless servers
- Document the 48-trigger limit with tables of working/failing patterns
- Explain Repetition optimization and why divisors of 60 work
- Add workaround examples (restrict hours, use divisor steps)
- Document Windows container limitation (Task Scheduler not available)
- Link to official Microsoft docs for CalendarTrigger, Repetition,
  LogonType, and security contexts
[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes
Fix CI: remove stale removeCrontabEntry ref, use verbose schtasks query

Remove silent SYSTEM fallback, fail with clear error instead

The automatic /ru SYSTEM retry silently escalated privileges when
SID resolution failed on headless servers. Instead, detect the error
and return a clear message explaining the issue with instructions
for manual workaround.

- Remove retryWithSystem() and tried_system_fallback field
- Detect "No mapping between account names" and provide actionable error
- Update test probe to not try SYSTEM fallback
- Update docs to explain the SID limitation without implying auto-fix

Fix scheduledTime example in cron docs to reflect Date.now() behavior

The example comment showed 1737340200000 (exactly 02:30:00.000 UTC),
implying scheduledTime is the precise scheduled fire time. The actual
implementation sets it to Date.now() at handler invocation, which
includes OS scheduler jitter and process startup overhead.

Add tests for cron docs claims that lacked direct coverage

Fills gaps between docs/runtime/cron.mdx and the test suite:
- Case-insensitive weekday/month names (docs L85)
- Relative path resolves against caller file, not cwd (docs L139)
- --cron-period carries normalized schedule into controller.cron (docs L157)
- Async handler rejection settles the wait, not just resolution (docs L164)
- Cartesian product dict count for multi-field patterns (docs L220)
- StandardOutPath/StandardErrorPath paths in plist (docs L231-232)
Add Bun.CronController type annotation to worker.ts example in cron docs

[autofix.ci] apply automated fixes

Merge branch 'main' of github.com:oven-sh/bun into ali/security-scanner-stdin
try ipc not bufer
try init as zero
Merge branch 'main' into ali/security-scanner-stdin

Refactor IPC handling in scanner-entry for Windows and POSIX

- Updated IPC message handling to differentiate between Windows and POSIX systems.
- Introduced `sendAndExit` function to streamline message sending and process termination.
- Enhanced error handling for package reading and JSON parsing.
- Adjusted `SecurityScanSubprocess` to utilize NODE_CHANNEL_FD for Windows IPC communication.

This refactor improves the robustness and clarity of the IPC implementation in the package manager.
try this on windows?
Merge branch 'main' into ali/security-scanner-stdin
fix on posix
comment
try to run on windows
Merge branch 'main' of github.com:oven-sh/bun into ali/security-scanner-stdin
Merge branch 'main' into ali/security-scanner-stdin
Merge branch 'main' into ali/security-scanner-stdin

Use inherited pipe for fd 4 instead of overlapped pipe

The previous approach used .buffer/.ipc stdio types for extra_fds, which
on Windows sets UV_OVERLAPPED_PIPE on the child's handle. That breaks
synchronous reads (fs.readSync, Bun.file().text()) in the child process.

Now both fd 3 and fd 4 use bun.sys.pipe() + inherit_fd, the same mechanism
that already works for fd 3. The parent writes JSON synchronously to fd 4's
write end then closes it; the child reads until EOF. No StaticPipeWriter
needed.

Also removed the branch-added SecurityScanSubprocessStaticPipeWriter poll
owner registration since we no longer use StaticPipeWriter.

Use StaticPipeWriter with asymmetric pipe flags for JSON write

The synchronous write deadlocked on Windows aarch64 when the pipe buffer
filled before the child started reading (>1MB payload, 60s timeout).

Fix: use StaticPipeWriter (async, event-loop driven) like Bun.spawn does
for blob stdin. The trick is creating the fd 4 pipe with asymmetric flags:

- Posix: .buffer stdio creates a nonblocking socketpair, both ends pollable
- Windows: uv_pipe(&fds, 0, UV_NONBLOCK_PIPE) makes only the write end
  overlapped. Child inherits the non-overlapped read end via .pipe
  (inherit_fd) so it can read synchronously; parent wraps the overlapped
  write end in a uv.Pipe via uv_pipe_open() for IOCP-based async writes.

This is what the branch was originally trying to do. All 11 previous
attempts used .buffer/.ipc stdio for extra_fds, which sets
UV_OVERLAPPED_PIPE on the child's handle (process.zig:1702) and breaks
synchronous reads in the child.

test: reduce package count in large-payload test for slower CI runners

10,000 packages meant 10,000 HTTP round-trips to the dummy registry,
which took >60s on Windows aarch64. Use 3000 packages with 150-char
padded names instead: same >1MB JSON, 3x fewer requests, 3x faster
(14s -> 4.5s on macOS). Also bump timeout to 120s for headroom.

Address review feedback: UAF fix + cleanup

- Track json_writer in remaining_fds (set to 2, decrement in onCloseIO,
  onProcessExit uses -=1 instead of =0). Prevents use-after-free if child
  crashes before json_writer completes: isDone() now waits for both reader
  and writer before allowing sleepUntil to return and the defer to free
  the struct.
- Use pipe.closeAndDestroy() instead of bun.destroy(pipe) in Windows
  errdefer. If pipe.init() succeeds but pipe.open() fails, the handle is
  registered in the uv loop; plain destroy leaves a dangling pointer.
- Remove stale __PACKAGES_JSON__ declaration from scanner-entry-globals.d.ts.

Skipped: the ref-count-leak comment was wrong. Writable.onClose sets the
tag to .ignore before finalize runs, so finalize's .buffer deref is dead
code in the normal flow. Double deref here causes SIGABRT.

Refactor: split spawn into spawnPosix/spawnWindows

The JsonInputPipe isomorphic struct was fake polymorphism — the posix
variant's closeChildEnd() was a no-op and create() just returned .buffer.
Split into two straightforward platform functions that each read
top-to-bottom as what actually happens. Common post-spawn setup
(reader, process, json writer, watch) lives in finishSpawn().

Address review: double-close, OOM ordering, extra_pipes leak

- spawnWindows: track fd ownership with optionals, null out after
  pipe.open() takes ownership and after explicit close. Prevents
  double-close on error paths (Windows HANDLE reuse = silent corruption).
- finishSpawn: move allocator.dupe() before ipc_reader.start() and
  setExitHandler(). If dupe OOMs, no event loop callbacks hold pointers
  to the struct yet, so the caller's bun.destroy is safe.
- Both spawn paths: defer spawned.extra_pipes.deinit(). The Managed
  ArrayList backing was leaked (pre-existing on main too).

Skipped the 'errdefer should deref twice' claim again — still causes
SIGABRT, same as before.

Free Process in cleanup defer (pre-existing leak from main)

toProcess() creates Process with ref=1, watch() bumps to 2, waiter
thread drops to 1 on exit. The owner ref was never released.

title: fix(sql): send Parse+Bind+Execute atomically for unnamed prepared statements
body:
## Summary

- **Fix PgBouncer incompatibility** with `prepare: false` (unnamed prepared statements) that could cause queries to silently execute against the wrong prepared statement, returning incorrect data or modifying wrong rows
- When `prepare: false` is used with parameterized queries, the driver previously split the extended query protocol into two round-trips (`Parse+Describe+Sync` → wait → `Bind+Execute+Sync`). The intermediate `Sync` causes a `ReadyForQuery` response, which PgBouncer interprets as the end of the transaction and releases the server connection. The subsequent `Bind+Execute` could then be routed to a different backend where the unnamed statement doesn't exist or was prepared with a different query
- Additionally, for repeated queries, the driver skipped `Parse` entirely and sent only `Bind+Execute`, assuming the unnamed statement still existed — incorrect when PgBouncer multiplexes connections
- Fix: for unnamed prepared statements, always send `Parse + [Describe] + Bind + Execute + Flush + Sync` as a single atomic message batch. Named prepared statements (`prepare: true`, the default) are completely unaffected

## Test plan

- [x] New `test/js/sql/sql-prepare-false.test.ts` with 8 tests covering parameterized queries, concurrent queries, transactions, and high concurrency with `prepare: false` — all pass
- [x] Existing SQL test suite (835 tests) — no regressions introduced (same pass/fail as system Bun)
